mnemonics.title: SCANLINE
mnemonics.content: S – Sort edges by y; C – Compute intersections; A – Add to active edge table; N – Next scanline; L – List intersections; I – Interpolate colors; N – Next pixel; E – End when no edges left.

mnemonics.title: FLOOD FILL
mnemonics.content: F – Find seed pixel; L – Load stack (or queue); O – Operate: pop pixel; O – Obtain its neighbors; D – Decide if inside; F – Fill pixel and push unfilled neighbors; I – Iterate until empty; L – Leave when boundary reached.

mnemonics.title: BOUNDARY FILL
mnemonics.content: B – Begin at seed; O – Obtain neighbors; U – Update if not boundary and not filled; N – Nearest pixel to boundary; D – Detect boundary color; A – Add to fill; R – Repeat until stack empty; Y – Yields filled region.

mnemonics.title: ACTIVE EDGE TABLE (AET)
mnemonics.content: A – Add edges starting at current scanline; C – Current scanline; T – Table holds active edges; I – Intersections sorted; O – Order updates each scanline; N – New edges added; E – Edges removed when done.

mnemonics.title: WINDING RULE
mnemonics.content: W – While traversing edges; I – Increase count for clockwise; D – Decrease for counter-clockwise; N – Non-zero total means inside; G – Given rule decides fill.

acronyms.usage: Maintains list of edges intersected by current scanline during scanline fill.
Pre‑processed bucket of edges sorted by their lower y‑coordinate.
Determines inside/outside based on edge direction (non‑zero or odd‑even).
Algorithm that fills polygon row by row using scanlines.
Recursive/iterative fill that stops when hitting a boundary color.
Algorithm to fill all connected pixels of same starting color.

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a horizontal laser line moving top‑to‑bottom across the polygon; wherever it cuts the polygon edges you light up the pixels between the intersection points.
Think of a filing cabinet with drawers labeled by y‑min; each drawer holds edges that start at that y coordinate.
A whiteboard that shows only the edges currently intersecting the scanline (like a shortlist) and updates each row.
Imagine a color‑filled region bounded by a wall; you drop a paint blob that spreads to neighboring cells but cannot cross the wall.
Think of a water spill on a floor with same‑color tiles; water spreads to any adjacent tile of the same color until it meets a different color.
Imagine walking along the polygon edges; each clockwise turn adds +1, each counter‑clockwise adds -1; the total tells you if you are inside.

analogies.analogy: Painting a fence stripe by stripe: you draw a horizontal line, fill the gap between posts, move down, repeat.
A sorted to‑do list of tasks (edges) grouped by the earliest start time.
A grocery list of items you currently need to buy, updated each time you move to the next aisle.
Filling a region in a coloring book with a marker: the marker spreads until it hits the drawn outline.
Spreading a puddle of water on a kitchen tile until it reaches a different colored tile.
A compass that counts left vs right turns; if the net turns are not zero, you are inside.
